subject: Air Law
question: Under what conditions may an RPL holder fly without further endorsement?
explanation: CASR 61.460(d) authorises an RPL holder to pilot only when the flight is conducted by day under the VFR. Night and IFR operations are outside RPL privileges.
reference: CASR 61.460 — Privileges of recreational pilot licences

subject: Air Law
question: The maximum certificated take-off weight for an aeroplane flown on RPL privileges is:
explanation: CASR 61.460(b) limits RPL operations to aircraft with a maximum certificated take-off weight not exceeding 1,500 kg.
reference: CASR 61.460 — Privileges of recreational pilot licences

subject: Air Law
question: Without a recreational navigation endorsement, an RPL holder may not fly beyond:
explanation: CASR 61.470(1)(a) restricts RPL operations without a navigation endorsement to within 25 NM of the departure aerodrome (or to a flight training area).
reference: CASR 61.470 — Limitations on exercise of privileges of recreational pilot licences—endorsements

subject: Air Law
question: An RPL holder without a controlled airspace endorsement wishes to enter Class C airspace. They:
option_a: may enter with an ATC clearance
option_b: may enter if they hold a flight radio endorsement
option_c: must remain clear of controlled airspace
option_d: may enter at or above 5,000 ft AMSL
explanation: CASR 61.470(2) requires a controlled airspace endorsement before an RPL holder may pilot an aircraft in controlled airspace. A clearance does not substitute for the endorsement.
reference: CASR 61.470 — Limitations on exercise of privileges of recreational pilot licences—endorsements

subject: Air Law
question: To take off or land at a controlled aerodrome, an RPL holder must hold a:
explanation: CASR 61.470(3) requires a controlled aerodrome endorsement for an RPL holder to operate at a controlled aerodrome.
reference: CASR 61.470 — Limitations on exercise of privileges of recreational pilot licences—endorsements

subject: Air Law
question: An RPL holder with a Class 2 medical certificate plans to carry two passengers on a private day VFR flight. They:
option_a: may not carry more than one passenger
option_c: must be accompanied by another pilot
option_d: must hold a controlled airspace endorsement
explanation: CASR 61.465(2) permits an RPL holder to carry more than one passenger when they hold a current Class 1 or Class 2 medical certificate. With a Class 2 medical the two-passenger limit does not apply.
reference: CASR 61.465 — Limitations on exercise of privileges of recreational pilot licences—general

subject: Air Law
question: An RPL holder without a Class 1 or Class 2 medical wishes to fly above 10,000 ft AMSL. They:
option_a: must hold a Class 1 or 2 medical
option_d: may do so by day VFR only
explanation: CASR 61.465(3) limits flight above 10,000 ft AMSL to RPL holders holding a Class 1 or 2 medical, or who are accompanied by another suitably qualified pilot.
reference: CASR 61.465 — Limitations on exercise of privileges of recreational pilot licences—general

subject: Air Law
question: Two aeroplanes are approaching head-on at the same altitude. Each pilot must alter heading to the:
explanation: CASR 91.330 and the additional rule for head-on encounters require each pilot to alter heading to the right.
reference: CASR 91.330 — Right of way rules; CASR 91.335 — Additional right of way rules

subject: Air Law
question: Two aeroplanes are converging at approximately the same altitude. Right of way belongs to the aircraft:
option_a: with the other on its right
option_b: with the other on its left
explanation: CASR 91.330 requires the aircraft that has another aircraft on its right to give way; the aircraft with the other on its left therefore has right of way.
reference: CASR 91.330 — Right of way rules

subject: Air Law
question: An aeroplane being overtaken by another aeroplane:
explanation: CASR 91.330 requires an overtaking aircraft to give way; the aircraft being overtaken has right of way.
reference: CASR 91.330 — Right of way rules

subject: Air Law
question: A power-driven aeroplane and an unpowered glider are converging at the same altitude. Right of way belongs to:
explanation: CASR 91.330 requires a power-driven aircraft to give way to an unpowered glider where their flight paths converge.
reference: CASR 91.330 — Right of way rules

subject: Air Law
question: Two heavier-than-air aircraft are on approach to land at the same aerodrome. Right of way belongs to the aircraft that is:
explanation: CASR 91.330 requires the higher of two heavier-than-air aircraft on approach to land to give way to the lower aircraft.
reference: CASR 91.330 — Right of way rules

subject: Air Law
question: An aircraft compelled to land in an emergency has:
option_c: no priority unless ATC declares so
option_d: right of way over all other aircraft
explanation: CASR 91.330 requires every other aircraft to give way to an aircraft that is in an emergency and compelled to land.
reference: CASR 91.330 — Right of way rules

subject: Air Law
question: Final authority over an aircraft during a flight rests with the:
explanation: CASR 91.215 places final authority over the aircraft with the pilot in command.
reference: CASR 91.215 — Authority and responsibilities of pilot in command

subject: Air Law
question: The pilot in command must comply with the:
explanation: CASR 91.095 requires the PIC to comply with the aircraft flight manual instructions and any conditions on the certificate of airworthiness.
reference: CASR 91.095 — Compliance with flight manual etc.

subject: Air Law
question: Before take-off, the pilot in command must check that the aircraft has:
option_d: a current MR or CRS is in force
explanation: Part 91 MOS 10.02(g) requires a check that a certificate of release to service or a maintenance release is in force before take-off.
reference: CASR 91.245 — Matters to be checked before take-off; Part 91 MOS section 10.02 — Matters to be checked before take-off

subject: Air Law
question: Responsibility for ensuring the aircraft is loaded within weight and balance limits rests with the:
explanation: CASR 91.215(2)(b) makes the pilot in command responsible for the safety of cargo on the aircraft.
reference: CASR 91.215 — Authority and responsibilities of pilot in command

subject: Air Law
question: A passenger refuses to fasten their seat belt for taxi. The pilot in command must:
option_a: depart and brief them in the air
option_c: ignore the refusal if the taxiway is short
explanation: CASR 91.220 authorises the pilot in command to direct any person on the aircraft regarding safety. The PIC must direct compliance before commencing the flight.
reference: CASR 91.220 — Actions and directions by operator or pilot in command

subject: Air Law
question: Becoming aware of a hazard to air navigation not in published information, the pilot in command must:
option_a: log it in the journey log
option_b: advise the operator by end of day
explanation: CASR 91.675 requires the PIC to report hazards to air navigation as soon as circumstances permit.
reference: CASR 91.675 — Pilot in command to report hazards to air navigation

subject: Air Law
question: An aircraft is in the vicinity of a non-controlled aerodrome when it is within:
option_a: 5 NM and at or below 3,000 ft AGL
option_b: 25 NM and at or below 5,000 ft AGL
option_c: 10 NM and at or below 3,000 ft AGL
option_d: 10 NM and at or below 5,000 ft AGL
explanation: CASR 91.360 defines an aircraft as in the vicinity of a non-controlled aerodrome when within 10 NM and at or below 3,000 ft AGL.
reference: CASR 91.360 — Meaning of in the vicinity of a non-controlled aerodrome

subject: Air Law
question: Standard circuit direction at an Australian non-controlled aerodrome is:
explanation: AIP ENR 1.1 §9.4 specifies left-hand circuits at non-controlled aerodromes unless an alternative direction is published in the aeronautical information for that aerodrome.
reference: AIP ENR 1.1 section 9.2 — Circuit Information

subject: Air Law
question: A pilot in command takes off from a non-controlled aerodrome. They contravene CASR if they begin the take-off when:
option_a: the wind is across the runway
option_c: a conflicting aircraft is on final
explanation: CASR 91.370 prohibits take-off or landing at a non-controlled aerodrome if a conflict with another aircraft exists.
reference: CASR 91.370 — Take-off or landing at non-controlled aerodrome—all aircraft

subject: Air Law
question: An RPL pilot may operate at a controlled aerodrome only when in compliance with:
explanation: CASR 91.405 governs the operation of aircraft in aerodrome traffic at controlled aerodromes; pilots must comply with ATC clearances and instructions.
reference: CASR 91.405 — Aircraft in aerodrome traffic at controlled aerodromes

subject: Air Law
question: An aerodrome may be used for a flight operation only if it is:
explanation: CASR 91.410 requires an aerodrome be approved for the type of operation conducted there.
reference: CASR 91.410 — Use of aerodromes

subject: Air Law
question: The minimum in-flight visibility for VFR in Class G at or below 3,000 ft AMSL or 1,000 ft AGL is:
explanation: Part 91 MOS Table 2.07(3) item 4 prescribes 5,000 m flight visibility, clear of cloud, in sight of ground or water for an aircraft in Class G at or below 3,000 ft AMSL or 1,000 ft AGL (whichever is higher).
reference: Part 91 MOS Chapter 2 Division 2.4 section 2.07 — VMC criteria

subject: Air Law
question: VFR cloud separation in Class G at 4,500 ft AMSL is:
option_b: 500 ft below, 1,000 ft above, 1 NM horizontal
explanation: Part 91 MOS Table 2.07(3) item 2 prescribes 1,500 m horizontal and 1,000 ft vertical from cloud for VFR aircraft in Class A, B, C, E or G below 10,000 ft AMSL above the lower-level allowances.
reference: Part 91 MOS Chapter 2 Division 2.4 section 2.07 — VMC criteria

subject: Air Law
question: At or above 10,000 ft AMSL, the VFR minimum flight visibility is:
explanation: Part 91 MOS Table 2.07(3) item 1 prescribes 8,000 m flight visibility for aircraft at or above 10,000 ft AMSL in Class A, B, C, E or G airspace.
reference: Part 91 MOS Chapter 2 Division 2.4 section 2.07 — VMC criteria

subject: Air Law
question: A VFR aeroplane is in Class G at 2,500 ft AMSL and 900 ft AGL over rising terrain. The applicable VMC minima are:
option_a: 5,000 m, clear of cloud, in sight of ground or water
option_b: 5,000 m, 1,500 m horizontal and 1,000 ft vertical from cloud
option_c: 8,000 m, 1,500 m horizontal and 1,000 ft vertical from cloud
explanation: Part 91 MOS Table 2.07(3) item 4 applies because the aircraft is at or below the higher of 3,000 ft AMSL or 1,000 ft AGL: 5,000 m flight visibility, clear of cloud, in sight of ground or water.
reference: Part 91 MOS Chapter 2 Division 2.4 section 2.07 — VMC criteria

subject: Air Law
question: VMC criteria in Class D airspace require:
explanation: Part 91 MOS Table 2.07(3) item 3 sets Class D VMC at 5,000 m flight visibility, 600 m horizontal, 1,000 ft above and 500 ft below cloud.
reference: Part 91 MOS Chapter 2 Division 2.4 section 2.07 — VMC criteria

subject: Air Law
question: The minimum height for an aeroplane over a populous area is:
explanation: CASR 91.265(2) prohibits flight below 1,000 ft above the highest feature or obstacle within 600 m of the aircraft.
reference: CASR 91.265 — Minimum height rules—populous areas and public gatherings

subject: Air Law
question: Over an area other than a populous area or public gathering, the minimum height is:
explanation: CASR 91.267(2) prohibits flight below 500 ft above the highest feature or obstacle within 600 m of the aircraft over non-populous areas.
reference: CASR 91.267 — Minimum height rules—other areas

subject: Air Law
question: Minimum height rules over a populous area do not apply when the aircraft is:
option_c: taking off or landing as prescribed
explanation: CASR 91.265(4)(a) and Part 91 MOS 12.01 exempt an aircraft taking off or landing in the prescribed circumstances.
reference: CASR 91.265 — Minimum height rules—populous areas and public gatherings; Part 91 MOS section 12.01 — Minimum height rules — populous areas and public gatherings

subject: Air Law
question: Over a populous area the 1,000 ft minimum is measured above:
explanation: CASR 91.265(2)(a) measures the 1,000 ft from the highest feature or obstacle within 600 m of the aircraft's ground track.
reference: CASR 91.265 — Minimum height rules—populous areas and public gatherings

subject: Air Law
question: An aeroplane is over a beach lined with houses at 700 ft AGL. This is:
option_d: unlawful — populous minima are not met
explanation: A beach lined with houses meets the populous-area test. CASR 91.265(2) requires 1,000 ft above the highest feature within 600 m radius; 700 ft does not meet that.
reference: CASR 91.265 — Minimum height rules—populous areas and public gatherings

subject: Air Law
question: Australian uncontrolled airspace is designated as:
explanation: AIP ENR 1.4 §3 classifies all uncontrolled airspace in Australia as Class G. Classes A, C, D and E are controlled.
reference: AIP ENR 1.4 section 3 — Class G Airspace

subject: Air Law
question: Class A airspace in Australia is available to:
explanation: Class A is reserved for IFR operations. CASR 91.285 confirms that VFR flight in Class A requires CASA approval; AIP ENR 1.4 §1 lists Class A as IFR-only airspace.
reference: CASR 91.285 — VFR flights—flights in class A airspace; AIP ENR 1.4 section 1 — Controlled Airspace

subject: Air Law
question: In Class C airspace, a VFR flight receives from ATC:
option_c: separation from IFR, info on VFR
explanation: AIP ENR 1.4 §1 provides that Class C separates IFR from VFR, and provides traffic information on other VFR.
reference: AIP ENR 1.4 section 1 — Controlled Airspace

subject: Air Law
question: VFR flights in Class E airspace in Australia:
explanation: AIP ENR 1.4 §2 states VFR flights in Class E do not require an ATC clearance; only IFR aircraft are separated and cleared.
reference: AIP ENR 1.4 section 2 — Class E Airspace

subject: Air Law
question: An RPL holder without endorsements plans to fly in Class G near a Class D control zone. They must:
option_a: obtain a clearance to operate within 5 NM
option_b: remain clear of the control zone
explanation: Without a controlled aerodrome endorsement, CASR 61.470(3) prohibits the RPL holder from operating at a controlled aerodrome; they must remain clear of the Class D control zone.
reference: CASR 61.470 — Limitations on exercise of privileges of recreational pilot licences—endorsements; AIP ENR 1.4 section 1.3 — Control Zone

subject: Air Law
question: The transponder code for a general emergency is:
explanation: Squawk 7700 is the worldwide ICAO transponder code for emergency. Squawk 7600 indicates radio failure, 7500 indicates unlawful interference.
reference: AIP ENR 1.6 — ATS Surveillance Services and Procedures

subject: Air Law
question: Following total radio failure, the transponder should be set to:
explanation: Squawk 7600 indicates radio communication failure. AIP ENR 1.6 publishes the transponder codes used in Australia.
reference: AIP ENR 1.6 — ATS Surveillance Services and Procedures

subject: Air Law
question: Transponder code 7500 indicates:
explanation: Squawk 7500 indicates unlawful interference (hijack). Squawk 7700 = general emergency, 7600 = radio failure.
reference: AIP ENR 1.6 — ATS Surveillance Services and Procedures

subject: Air Law
question: The SAR phase declared when an aircraft is reasonably certain to be in distress is:
explanation: AIP GEN 3.6 defines DETRESFA as the distress phase, declared when reasonable certainty exists that an aircraft and its occupants are in grave and imminent danger. INCERFA = uncertainty, ALERFA = alert.
reference: AIP GEN 3.6 — Search and Rescue

subject: Air Law
question: A pilot encountering grave and imminent danger transmits a radio call prefixed:
option_a: PAN PAN, PAN PAN, PAN PAN
explanation: CASR 91.700 prescribes use of the distress signal MAYDAY (spoken three times) when the aircraft is in grave and imminent danger. PAN PAN is the urgency signal.
reference: CASR 91.700 — Aviation distress signals

subject: Air Law
question: Which document is NOT required to be carried on board for a private VFR flight by day within 50 NM of departure?
explanation: CASR 91.105(2) lists the licence, medical and AFM instructions among required documents; the personal logbook is not among them. CASR 61.345 governs logbook upkeep but does not require it on board.
reference: CASR 91.105 — Carriage of documents

subject: Air Law
question: The pilot in command must carry their flight crew licence and medical certificate:
option_a: on board the aircraft for every flight
option_d: only when more than 50 NM from departure
explanation: CASR 91.105(2)(a) requires both the medical certificate and the flight crew licence to be carried on the aircraft for each flight; CASR 61.420 mirrors this requirement for licence holders.
reference: CASR 91.105 — Carriage of documents; CASR 61.420 — Limitations on exercise of privileges of pilot licences—carriage of documents

subject: Air Law
question: The aircraft flight manual instructions for the aircraft must be:
option_d: carried on the aircraft for the flight
explanation: CASR 91.105(2)(c) requires the aircraft flight manual instructions to be carried on the aircraft when the flight begins.
reference: CASR 91.105 — Carriage of documents

subject: Air Law
question: Before a private VFR cross-country flight beyond 50 NM, the PIC must additionally carry:
option_a: a printed copy of the AIP
option_b: authorised aero info and the MR
explanation: CASR 91.110 requires the authorised aeronautical information for the flight and the maintenance release to be carried for flights other than day VFR within 50 NM of departure.
reference: CASR 91.110 — Carriage of documents for certain flights

subject: Air Law
question: From a control tower to an aircraft in flight, a steady green light means:
option_a: return to starting point on aerodrome
option_c: authorised to land if no conflict
explanation: AIP ENR 1.5 §12.1 states a steady green to an aircraft in flight means authorised to land if no collision risk exists.
reference: CASR 91.670 — Standard visual signals; AIP ENR 1.5 section 12.1 — Light Signals to Aircraft

subject: Air Law
question: From a control tower to an aircraft on the aerodrome, a steady red light means:
option_b: taxi clear of landing area in use
option_c: return to starting point on aerodrome
explanation: AIP ENR 1.5 §12.1 states that a steady red light directed at an aircraft on the aerodrome means stop.
reference: CASR 91.670 — Standard visual signals; AIP ENR 1.5 section 12.1 — Light Signals to Aircraft

subject: Air Law
question: Red flashes directed at an aircraft in flight mean:
explanation: AIP ENR 1.5 §12.1 indicates red flashes to an aircraft in flight mean the aerodrome is unsafe — do not land.
reference: CASR 91.670 — Standard visual signals; AIP ENR 1.5 section 12.1 — Light Signals to Aircraft

subject: Air Law
question: On the aerodrome, green flashes from the tower mean:
option_b: authorised to taxi if pilot satisfied no collision risk exists
option_c: return to starting point on aerodrome
option_d: authorised to take-off if pilot satisfied no collision risk exists
explanation: AIP ENR 1.5 §12.1 states green flashes on the aerodrome mean authorised to taxi if the pilot is satisfied no collision risk exists.
reference: CASR 91.670 — Standard visual signals; AIP ENR 1.5 section 12.1 — Light Signals to Aircraft

subject: Air Law
question: Before an Australian aircraft may begin a flight, a maintenance release (or approved alternative) must be:
option_a: signed by the pilot before start-up
explanation: CASR 91.145(2)(d) requires a maintenance release (or an approved alternative document) to be in force for the aircraft and for the flight to comply with any condition on it.
reference: CASR 91.145 — Requirements to be met before Australian aircraft may fly

subject: Air Law
question: Before the first flight of the day, the matters to be checked by the pilot in command include:
option_c: CRS or MR is in force
explanation: Part 91 MOS 10.02(g) requires a check for a certificate of release to service or a maintenance release before take-off.
reference: CASR 91.245 — Matters to be checked before take-off; Part 91 MOS section 10.02 — Matters to be checked before take-off

subject: Air Law
question: Authorised aeronautical information and the maintenance release must be carried on board for:
option_b: other than day VFR within 50 NM
explanation: CASR 91.110 applies to flights other than day VFR within 50 NM of the departure point or a flying training area.
reference: CASR 91.110 — Carriage of documents for certain flights

subject: Air Law
question: A defect that may affect the safe operation of the aircraft is found in flight. The pilot in command must:
option_c: divert immediately to the nearest aerodrome
option_d: assess and not continue if safety affected
explanation: CASR 91.215 places responsibility for the safe operation of the aircraft with the pilot in command. The PIC must assess any in-flight defect and stop the flight (or take other safe action) if it affects safe operation. Immediate diversion to the nearest suitable aerodrome is mandated only when the situation amounts to an emergency under CASR 91.680.
reference: CASR 91.215 — Authority and responsibilities of pilot in command

subject: Air Law
question: Operating below 10,000 ft AMSL on an en route segment in Class G, the altimeter must be set to:
option_c: aerodrome QNH from the departure airfield
explanation: Part 91 MOS 11.02(5) requires altimeter settings below the transition altitude (10,000 ft) to be the current local QNH within 100 NM of the aircraft, or the current forecast area QNH.
reference: Part 91 MOS section 11.02 — Transition altitude, transition layer and transition level; AIP ENR 1.7 section 2 — Basic altimeter setting procedures

subject: Air Law
question: On climb, when the aircraft passes the transition altitude the altimeter must be changed from:
explanation: Part 91 MOS 11.02(7) requires the altimeter to be changed from QNH to 1013.2 hPa after passing the transition altitude, before levelling off in the transition layer.
reference: Part 91 MOS section 11.02 — Transition altitude, transition layer and transition level; AIP ENR 1.7 section 2 — Basic altimeter setting procedures

subject: Air Law
question: Before take-off the PIC of a VFR flight may consider a pressure altitude system operative only if, with an accurate QNH set, it reads the site elevation to within:
explanation: Part 91 MOS 10.05(1) permits a pressure altitude system for VFR flight only if it reads site elevation to within 100 ft (or 110 ft above 3,300 ft).
reference: Part 91 MOS section 10.05 — Checking pressure altitude systems — VFR flight

subject: Air Law
question: Above the transition altitude an aircraft's altimeter setting must be:
explanation: Part 91 MOS 11.02(6) requires altimeter setting of 1013.2 hPa for an operation above the transition altitude, giving a flight level indication.
reference: Part 91 MOS section 11.02 — Transition altitude, transition layer and transition level

subject: Air Law
question: Joining the circuit at a non-controlled aerodrome by overflying should be conducted at:
option_b: overfly 500 ft above, descend non-traffic side
explanation: AIP ENR 1.1 §9.12 requires overflying at least 500 ft above circuit height, descending on the non-traffic side to circuit height before joining crosswind.
reference: AIP ENR 1.1 section 9.12 — Circuit Entry

subject: Air Law
question: Standard circuit height for fixed-wing aeroplanes at most Australian non-controlled aerodromes is:
explanation: AIP ENR 1.1 §9.4 prescribes the standard circuit height as 1,000 ft AGL for aeroplanes unless a different circuit height is published for the aerodrome.
reference: AIP ENR 1.1 section 9.4 — Circuit Height

subject: Air Law
question: On final approach to a non-controlled aerodrome a pilot observes another aircraft already established on a straight-in approach. The correct action is to:
option_b: call the other aircraft to hold
option_d: give way to the established aircraft
explanation: CASR 91.395 and AIP ENR 1.1 §9.13 require the pilot joining the circuit to give way to an aircraft already established on a straight-in approach.
reference: CASR 91.395 — Straight-in approaches at non-controlled aerodromes; AIP ENR 1.1 section 9.13 — Straight-in Approach

subject: Air Law
question: The final reserve fuel for a private day VFR piston aeroplane of 1,500 kg MTOW is:
explanation: Part 91 MOS Table 19.02(2) item 1 prescribes 30 minutes final reserve fuel for a piston aeroplane of MTOW ≤ 5,700 kg on a VFR flight.
reference: CASR 91.455 — Fuel requirements; Part 91 MOS section 19.02 — Definitions of final reserve fuel and contingency fuel

subject: Air Law
question: Trip fuel for a flight must be sufficient to reach the:
explanation: Part 91 MOS 19.04(1)(b) defines trip fuel as the fuel required to reach the destination aerodrome. Final reserve, contingency and alternate fuel are separate components.
reference: CASR 91.455 — Fuel requirements; Part 91 MOS section 19.04 — Amount of fuel that must be carried for a flight

subject: Air Law
question: If the PIC determines that the aircraft will land with less than final reserve fuel, they must broadcast:
explanation: Part 91 MOS 19.06(4) requires the pilot in command to declare a situation of "emergency fuel" by broadcasting "MAYDAY, MAYDAY, MAYDAY FUEL" when, at any time during a flight, the aircraft will land at the nearest aerodrome where a safe landing can be made with less than final reserve fuel remaining.
reference: CASR 91.455 — Fuel requirements; Part 91 MOS section 19.06 — Procedures if fuel reaches specified amounts

subject: Air Law
question: Before take-off, passengers must be briefed on:
explanation: Part 91 MOS 20.06 lists briefing items including seatbelt use, emergency exit location/use, and how and when to adopt the brace position.
reference: CASR 91.565 — Passengers—safety briefings and instructions; Part 91 MOS section 20.06 — Passenger safety briefings and instructions

subject: Air Law
question: For a flight where life jackets are carried, the briefing must include:
option_a: where the jackets are and how to use them
option_c: the survival time in cold water
option_d: that jackets must be inflated before departure
explanation: Part 91 MOS 20.06(m)-(n) require the briefing to include the location and use of life jackets, and that jackets must not be inflated inside the aircraft.
reference: CASR 91.565 — Passengers—safety briefings and instructions; Part 91 MOS section 20.06 — Passenger safety briefings and instructions

subject: Air Law
question: Smoking on board a small aeroplane is:
option_c: permitted unless a sign is lit
option_d: not during take-off or landing or when PIC directs
explanation: CASR 91.530 prohibits smoking during take-off or landing, during any period the PIC directs no smoking, and at all times in the aircraft's toilet.
reference: CASR 91.530 — When smoking not permitted

subject: Air Law
question: Aerobatic manoeuvres may only be conducted in accordance with:
option_d: the CASR 91 rule and any approval
explanation: CASR 91.185 governs the conduct of aerobatic manoeuvres, prescribing the requirements and any approvals needed.
reference: CASR 91.185 — Conducting aerobatic manoeuvres

subject: Air Law
question: Formation flight in Australia is permitted when:
option_a: each PIC agrees and rules are met
option_b: the lead aircraft holds an IR
option_d: only the lead is formation qualified
explanation: CASR 91.205 and Part 91 MOS Chapter 6 require each PIC to agree to the formation and for the prescribed formation-flight rules to be complied with.
reference: CASR 91.205 — Flying in formation; Part 91 MOS Chapter 6 — Flying in Formation

subject: Air Law
question: Dropping an object from an aircraft in flight is:
explanation: CASR 91.190 prohibits dropping things from an aircraft unless the regulation's conditions are met.
reference: CASR 91.190 — Dropping things from aircraft

subject: Air Law
question: For Australian day VFR operations, daylight is the period:
option_b: beginning of morning civil twilight to end of evening civil twilight
option_d: 15 min before sunrise to 15 min after sunset
explanation: AIP GEN 2.2 and the VFRG Glossary define day as the period from the beginning of morning civil twilight to the end of evening civil twilight.
reference: AIP GEN 2.2 — Definitions and Abbreviations; VFRG Glossary

subject: Air Law
question: An RPL pilot plans a VFR flight expected to land 10 minutes after the end of evening civil twilight. The flight:
option_a: is permitted provided landing lights are on
option_b: is permitted within 25 NM of departure
option_d: is permitted if the pilot holds a night VFR rating
explanation: CASR 61.460(d) restricts RPL privileges to flight conducted by day under the VFR. Landing 10 minutes after the end of evening civil twilight is outside day operations and contravenes RPL privileges.
reference: CASR 61.460 — Privileges of recreational pilot licences

subject: Air Law
question: To carry a passenger by day, a pilot must have completed in the previous 90 days at least:
option_c: 3 take-offs and 3 landings, day or night
explanation: CASR 61.395(1) requires 3 take-offs and 3 landings in the previous 90 days, by day or night; each take-off must be followed by a climb to at least 500 ft AGL.
reference: CASR 61.395 — Limitations on exercise of privileges of pilot licences—recent experience for certain passenger flight activities

subject: Air Law
question: A pilot's flight review expired two weeks ago. They wish to fly as pilot in command. They may:
option_b: not exercise privileges until a review
option_d: fly for training with any pilot
explanation: CASR 61.400 requires a current flight review; once expired the pilot must not exercise licence privileges until a flight review is completed.
reference: CASR 61.400 — Limitations on exercise of privileges of pilot licences—flight review

subject: Air Law
question: At a non-controlled aerodrome, a pilot must not commence take-off until:
explanation: AIP ENR 1.1 §9.3 sets take-off separation at a non-controlled aerodrome; CASR 91.370 makes a conflict take-off an offence.
reference: CASR 91.370 — Take-off or landing at non-controlled aerodrome—all aircraft; AIP ENR 1.1 section 9.3 — Separation Minima for Take-off

subject: Air Law
question: You are lined up for take-off at a non-controlled aerodrome. An aircraft is on final approach 1 NM out. You may:
option_b: not depart until the landing aircraft clears
option_c: depart if the other pilot sees you
explanation: CASR 91.340 gives a landing aircraft right of way over one preparing to take off; CASR 91.370 prohibits a conflicting take-off.
reference: CASR 91.340 — Right of way rules for take-off and landing; CASR 91.370 — Take-off or landing at non-controlled aerodrome—all aircraft

subject: Air Law
question: A passenger consumes alcohol on board without the pilot in command's permission. This is:
option_b: permitted if the passenger is not seated next to the pilot
option_d: permitted if the alcohol was purchased duty-free
explanation: CASR 91.780(1) makes it a contravention for a passenger to consume alcohol on board unless a crew member has provided it, or, where the PIC is the only crew, the PIC has permitted it.
reference: CASR 91.780 — Passengers—alcohol

subject: Air Law
question: If a crew member has reasonable grounds to believe a person is affected by a psychoactive substance so that they may be a hazard, the person may be:
option_b: seated only in the rear of the aircraft
option_c: carried if the PIC gives consent
explanation: CASR 91.790 authorises the operator or a crew member to prohibit a person from boarding if they have reasonable grounds to believe the person is affected by a psychoactive substance to an extent that may present a hazard.
reference: CASR 91.790 — Prohibiting person affected by psychoactive substances from boarding
